courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: At least 70% in NSC Mathematics. Students in other faculties not meeting the 70% NSC requirement may instead complete MAM1014F/MAM1022F followed by MAM1015S with a mark of 70% or higher. From 2027, this may be replaced by MAM1025F/MAM1026F followed by MAM1015S with 70% or higher.
courses.course_outline: This course provides mathematics for applications, particularly in the Life and Earth sciences. Topics include: functions and graphs; straight lines, power functions, polynomials, exponential and logarithmic functions, trigonometric functions (radians); discrete-time dynamical systems; stability and equilibria; rates of change; limits, derivatives; maxima and minima; concavity; asymptotes and curve sketching; antiderivatives and integrals; mathematical modelling; separable and linear differential equations.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 1st period.
courses.dp_requirements: A class record of 30% or more; satisfactory lecture and tutorial attendance.
courses.assessment: Class tests and regular assignments determine a class record; one 3-hour examination makes up the balance of the final mark.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: At least 70% in NSC Mathematics. Students in other faculties not meeting the 70% NSC requirement may instead complete MAM1014F/MAM1022F followed by MAM1015S with a mark of 70% or higher. From 2027, this may be replaced by MAM1025F/MAM1026F followed by MAM1015S with 70% or higher.
courses.course_outline: As per MAM1004F. The aim of this course is to provide mathematics for applications, particularly in the Life and Earth sciences. Topics include: functions and graphs; straight lines, power functions, polynomials, exponential and logarithmic functions, trigonometric functions (radians); discrete-time dynamical systems; stability and equilibria; rates of change; limits, derivatives; maxima and minima; concavity; asymptotes and curve sketching; antiderivatives and integrals; mathematical modelling; separable and linear differential equations.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, Meridian.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 30% in class tests, and at least 80% attendance at tutorials.
courses.assessment: Year mark counts up to 40%; one 3-hour examination makes up the balance.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: At least 70% in NSC Mathematics. Permission of the Dean or Head of Department required prior to registration. Note: MAM1005H + MAM1006H is equivalent to MAM1031F + MAM1032S in level, credit value and as prerequisite for other courses. The course emphasises strengthening foundational concepts and skills and carefully-paced introduction of new material.
courses.course_outline: Similar to MAM1031F, this course introduces the fundamental ideas in calculus and related topics. It covers the topics in MAM1031F, including differential calculus of functions of one variable, but extended over the full year.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 1st or 3rd period (depending on timetable). Workshops: Monday, 6th and 7th period.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 35% for class record and very satisfactory attendance at all lectures, workshops and tutorials.
courses.assessment: Year mark counts up to 50%; one 2-hour examination written in October/November makes up the balance.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM1005H or a pass with at least 65% in MAM1004F/S. Students who passed MAM1004F/S with less than 65% must write and pass the examination paper for MAM1005H before registering. Note: MAM1005H + MAM1006H is equivalent to MAM1000W in level, credit value and as prerequisite for certain courses.
courses.course_outline: Similar to MAM1000W, this course introduces the fundamental ideas in calculus, linear algebra and related topics. It covers those topics in the MAM1000W syllabus not covered in MAM1005H, including differential equations, partial derivatives, vector geometry, matrix algebra, complex numbers and Taylor series.
courses.lecture_times: Lectures Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday and Friday in first period. Tutorials Thursday in first period. No workshops.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 35% in class tests and very satisfactory attendance at lectures and tutorials.
courses.assessment: Year mark counts up to 40%; one 2-hour examination written in October/November makes up the balance.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.co_requisites: MAM1004S or MAM1005H (unless a pass has been obtained for MAM1004F, MAM1031F or equivalent).
courses.course_outline: This course provides a foundation for Computer Science and Applied Statistics, covering discrete structures including set theory, logic, graph theory and probability theory. Topics include: (1) sets, relations and functions; (2) basic logic including propositional logic, logical connectives, truth tables, propositional inference rules and predicate logic; (3) proof techniques including direct proofs, disproving by counterexample and proof by contradiction; (4) basics of counting including the pigeonhole principle, permutations, combinations and recurrence relations; (5) graphs and trees; (6) discrete probability including finite probability space, axioms of probability and conditional probability; and (7) linear algebra including vectors, matrices and their applications. The course is offered in a blended-learning format with online video lectures and tutorial-format contact sessions.
courses.lecture_times: No face-to-face lectures. Course content is delivered online.
courses.dp_requirements: Class Record of at least 30% and attendance at 10 or more (out of 12) tutorials.
courses.assessment: Class Record counts 50%; exam counts 50%.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: At least 70% in NSC Mathematics.
courses.course_outline: The aim of this course is to familiarise students with the most fundamental concepts and tools of modern mathematics at an elementary level. Topics include: fundamentals of logic and set theory; concepts of a function; relations; equivalence and order relations; some basic mathematical structures; and the fundamental number systems. Students who intend to major in mathematics are expected to take this course during their first year.
courses.lecture_times: Five lectures every two weeks in meridian.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 30% in year mark and satisfactory tutorial attendance.
courses.assessment: Year mark counts 30–40%; one 2-hour examination written in June/July and one in October/November equally make up the balance.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: At least 70% in NSC Mathematics.
courses.course_outline: The aim of this course is to introduce students to the fundamental ideas in differential calculus covering functions of one variable, limits, continuity and differentiation with applications, as well as formal proof methods. This course (or equivalent), along with MAM1032S (or equivalent), is necessary for entry into second-year mathematics.
courses.lecture_times: Five lectures per week, Monday - Friday, 1st or 3rd period.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 30% for class tests; minimum 30% for weekly online tests; 80% attendance at tutorial sessions.
courses.assessment: Semester mark counts 33.3%; end-of-semester exam counts 66.6%.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM1031F or MAM1033F or MAM1004F (with 65% or higher).
courses.course_outline: This course continues from MAM1031F and introduces integral calculus, Taylor polynomials, complex numbers, vector geometry, linear algebra and differential equations. This course, along with MAM1031F, is necessary for entry into second-year mathematics.
courses.lecture_times: Five lectures per week, Monday - Friday, 1st or 3rd period.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 30% for class tests; minimum 30% for weekly online tests; 80% attendance at tutorial sessions.
courses.assessment: Semester mark counts 33.3%; end-of-semester exam counts 66.6%.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: At least 70% in NSC Mathematics.
courses.course_outline: The aim of this course is to introduce the fundamental ideas in differential calculus, designed for students majoring in mathematical and physical sciences. A fundamental aim is for students to understand how to read, write and apply mathematics using mathematical thinking practices for clarity, consistency and depth of understanding that prepares them for MAM2000W, MAM3000W, and the mathematical requirements of their other Science courses.
courses.lecture_times: Five lectures per week, Monday - Friday, 1st or 3rd period.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 30% for class tests; minimum 30% for weekly online tests; 80% attendance at tutorial sessions; 100% attendance at Mathematical Thinking workshops.
courses.assessment: Semester mark counts 33.3%; end-of-semester exam counts 66.6%.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM1033F.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces the fundamental ideas in calculus and related topics. Main topics include integral calculus, differential equations, partial derivatives, vector geometry, matrix algebra, complex numbers and Taylor polynomials. The course includes an explicit focus on mathematical thinking practices including inductive and deductive reasoning, justifying, defining, proving, using mathematical understanding to flexibly solve multi-level problems, reading mathematics for learning, and communicating mathematical knowledge in writing. This course (or equivalent), along with MAM1033F (or equivalent), is necessary for entry into second-year mathematics.
courses.lecture_times: Five lectures per week, Monday - Friday, 1st or 3rd period.
courses.dp_requirements: Minimum of 30% for class tests; minimum 30% for weekly online tests; 80% attendance at tutorial sessions; 100% attendance at Mathematical Thinking workshops.
courses.assessment: Semester mark counts 33.3%; end-of-semester exam counts 66.6%.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.course_outline: The aim of this course is to introduce Applied Mathematics and Mathematical Modelling through approximations and estimation theory, dimensional analysis, numerical methods, rudimentary modelling using first- and second-order ordinary differential equations, discrete- and continuous-time dynamical systems, and modelling and simulation of discrete- and continuous-time processes with MATLAB.
courses.lecture_times: First Semester: 2nd period Monday, Wednesday, Friday. Second Semester: 2nd period Tuesday, Thursday.
courses.notes: Can be taken in conjunction with MAM1044H as lectures are arranged so that this is possible. Each student is required to have a laptop for use during class sessions and after hours.
courses.dp_requirements: A class record of 30% or more; satisfactory attendance at lectures and tutorials; submission of all assigned work.
courses.assessment: Class record counts 20–40%; one 3-hour examination written in October/November makes up the balance.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.course_outline: The aim of this course is to introduce the elements of mechanics. Topics include: kinematics in three dimensions; Newton's laws of motion; models of forces (friction, elastic springs, fluid resistance); conservation of energy and momentum; simple systems of particles, including a brief introduction to rigid systems; and orbital mechanics with applications to the planning of space missions to the outer planets.
courses.lecture_times: First semester: 2nd period Tuesday, Thursday. Second semester: 2nd period Monday, Wednesday, Friday.
courses.notes: Can be taken in conjunction with MAM1043H as lectures are arranged so that this is possible.
courses.dp_requirements: A class record of 30% or more.
courses.assessment: Class record counts up to 50%; the balance is made up by the final examination.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM1031F and MAM1032S or equivalent. With convener permission, students with 70% for both MAM1010F and MAM1012S may register.
courses.course_outline: Students study the fundamentals of multivariable calculus, including: curves and surfaces in three dimensions; change of coordinates; line integrals; surface integrals; and Stokes's, Green's and divergence theorems.
courses.lecture_times: Tuesdays, Fridays and some Wednesdays in 5th period. Note: Lectures alternate during the week so students can take MAM2010F and MAM2011F concurrently.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM1031F and MAM1032S or equivalent. With convener permission, students with 70% for both MAM1010F and MAM1012S may register.
courses.course_outline: Students study the fundamentals of linear algebra, including: vector spaces, linear independence, spans, bases, row space, column space, null space; linear maps; eigenvectors and eigenvalues; inner product spaces; and orthogonality.
courses.lecture_times: Mondays, Thursdays and some Wednesdays in 5th period. Note: Lectures alternate during the week so students can take MAM2010F and MAM2011F concurrently.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM1031F and MAM1032S or equivalent; MAM2010F and MAM2011F.
courses.course_outline: Students study the fundamentals of differential equations, including: first and second-order difference equations; linear differential equations with constant coefficients; Laplace transforms; nonlinear equations and phase plane analysis; parabolic partial differential equations, separation of variables and boundary value problems; Black-Scholes equation; stochastic differential equations.
courses.lecture_times: Tuesdays, Fridays and some Wednesdays in 4th period. Note: Lectures alternate during the week so students can take MAM2012S, MAM2013S and MAM2014S concurrently.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM1031F and MAM1032S or equivalent.
courses.course_outline: Students study the fundamentals of abstract algebra and number theory, including: induction, strong induction and the Well-Ordering axiom; divisibility and prime factorisation; modular arithmetic; permutations; groups, subgroups, cyclic groups; isomorphisms; simple groups, factor groups and Lagrange's Theorem; the First Isomorphism Theorem.
courses.lecture_times: Tuesdays, Fridays and some Wednesdays in 5th period. Note: Lectures alternate during the week so students can take MAM2012S, MAM2013S and MAM2014S concurrently.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM1031F and MAM1032S or equivalent.
courses.course_outline: Students study the fundamentals of real analysis, including: axioms of the real numbers, supremum and infimum; countable sets; sequences and series; open and closed sets, compactness; limits, continuity, differentiability; sequences and series of functions, uniform convergence, power series; integration.
courses.lecture_times: Mondays, Thursdays and some Wednesdays in 4th and 5th period. Note: Lectures alternate during the week so students can take MAM2012S, MAM2013S and MAM2014S concurrently.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM1031F and MAM1032S or equivalent; MAM1043H and MAM1044H.
courses.course_outline: Topics include: first-order equations; existence and uniqueness of solutions; linear equations of the n-th order; systems of n linear first-order equations; non-homogeneous linear equations and systems; variation of parameters; qualitative theory of non-linear equations; phase plane analysis; externally- and parametrically-driven oscillators; resonance; application to the theory of non-linear vibrations; and calculus of variations.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 3rd period.
courses.notes: Can be taken in conjunction with MAM2041F as lectures are arranged so that this is possible.
courses.dp_requirements: A class record of 30% or more; satisfactory attendance at lectures and tutorials; submission of all assigned work.
courses.assessment: Class record counts up to 50%; the balance is made up by the final examination.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM1031F and MAM1032S or equivalent; MAM1043H and MAM1044H.
courses.course_outline: Topics include: non-linear equations and rates of convergence; direct and iterative methods for solving linear systems, pivoting strategies, matrix factorisation, norms, conditioning; solutions to initial-value problems including higher-order ordinary differential equations; interpolation and approximation theory, splines, discrete and continuous least squares; numerical differentiation and integration; error analysis and control.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 3rd period.
courses.notes: Can be taken in conjunction with MAM2040F as lectures are arranged so that this is possible.
courses.dp_requirements: A class record of 30% or more.
courses.assessment: Class record counts up to 50%; the balance is made up by the final examination.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM1031F and MAM1032S or equivalent; MAM1043H; MAM1044H; MAM2010F and MAM2011F.
courses.course_outline: Topics include: fixed points, bifurcations, phase portraits; conservative and reversible systems; index theory, Poincaré–Bendixson theorem, Liénard systems, relaxation oscillators; Hopf bifurcations, quasiperiodicity and Poincaré maps; applications including chaos on a strange attractor, Lorentz map, logistic map, Hénon map; Lyapunov exponents; fractals.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 3rd period.
courses.notes: Can be taken in conjunction with MAM2043S as lectures are arranged so that this is possible.
courses.dp_requirements: A class record of 30% or more; satisfactory attendance at lectures and tutorials; submission of all assigned work.
courses.assessment: Class record counts up to 50%; the balance is made up by the final examination.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM1031F and MAM1032S or equivalent; MAM1043H; MAM1044H; MAM2010F; MAM2011F; and MAM2040F.
courses.course_outline: Topics include: homogeneous and non-homogeneous boundary-value problems; Sturm–Liouville problems; partial differential equations and their solution via separation of variables; Fourier series; Green's functions; eigenfunction expansions.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 3rd period.
courses.notes: Can be taken in conjunction with MAM2042S as lectures are arranged so that this is possible.
courses.dp_requirements: A class record of 30% or more; satisfactory attendance at lectures and tutorials; submission of all assigned work.
courses.assessment: Class record counts up to 50%; the balance is made up by the final examination.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.course_outline: With permission from the Convenor, and subject to the availability of a suitable supervisor in the Department, students may complete a project on a topic in Mathematics. This is strongly recommended for students intending to continue to Honours in Mathematics.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM2010F, MAM2011F, MAM2014S and at least one of MAM2012S or MAM2013S.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces fundamental concepts in metric spaces and topology. Topics include: metrics, ultrametrics, pseudometrics, quasi-metrics; convergence in metric spaces; closed sets and closures; open sets and interiors; definition of topology in metric space and in general; continuous, uniformly continuous, Lipschitz continuous, contractions, isometries functions; topological, uniform and metric isomorphisms; completeness via Cauchy sequences; Cantor's intersection theorem; construction and uniqueness of completion; Banach's Fixed Point Theorem and applications; compactness via convergent subsequences and equivalence with total boundedness, completeness and open cover property; connectedness, disconnectedness and connected components.
courses.lecture_times: Tuesdays, Fridays and some Wednesdays in 5th period.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM2010F, MAM2011F, MAM2013S and at least one of MAM2012S or MAM2014S.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces the following areas of Group Theory: the Isomorphism Theorems, p-Groups, Sylow Theory, Direct Products and finitely generated Abelian Groups.
courses.lecture_times: Mondays, Thursdays and some Wednesdays in 5th period.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM2010F, MAM2011F, and two of MAM2012S, MAM2013S and MAM2014S.
courses.course_outline: Graph theory, combinatorial counting, discrete probability theory, recurrences, algorithms and applications.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 4th period.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM2010F, MAM2011F, and two of MAM2012S, MAM2013S and MAM2014S.
courses.course_outline: This course provides an introduction to order and lattice theory. Topics include: lattices and order, congruences, distributivity and Boolean algebras, representation theory, naive set theory, and universal algebra.
courses.lecture_times: Tuesdays, Fridays and some Wednesdays in 5th period.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM3010F.
courses.course_outline: Topics include: (1) study of sequences of real numbers; (2) revision on metric spaces including distance, balls, open and closed sets, convergence, compactness, completeness, spaces of continuous functions, normed spaces, linear continuous mappings, equivalence of norms, Banach spaces and dual spaces; (3) Hilbert spaces including scalar product, orthogonal complement, orthogonal projection and the Gram-Schmidt orthonormalization process; (4) differential calculus between normed spaces including Gâteaux and Fréchet differentiability, chain rule, the Lagrange inequality, convexity, the implicit function theorem and the inverse mapping theorem; (5) an introduction to Riemann integration and its limitations.
courses.lecture_times: Mondays, Thursdays and some Wednesdays in 5th period.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM2010F, MAM2011F, MAM2014S and at least one of MAM2012S or MAM2013S.
courses.course_outline: Topics include: fields of complex numbers; holomorphic functions; power series; elementary functions; complex integration; Cauchy's Integral Theorem; Cauchy's Integral Formula; Liouville's Theorem; Fundamental Theorem of Algebra; Coincidence Principle; Maximum Modulus Principle; singularities; residues; Laurent Series; arguments; Rouché's Theorem; and Open Mapping Theorems.
courses.lecture_times: Tuesdays, Fridays and some Wednesdays in 4th period.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM2040F, MAM2041F, MAM2042S and MAM2043S.
courses.course_outline: Advanced methods for ODEs, boundary value problems and differential eigenvalue problems. Numerical solution of PDEs by methods of finite differences, finite elements and spectral methods.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 3rd period.
courses.notes: Can be taken in conjunction with MAM3044F as lectures are arranged so that this is possible.
courses.dp_requirements: A class record of 30% or more.
courses.assessment: Class record counts up to 20%; the balance is made up by the final examination.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM2040F, MAM2041F, MAM2042S and MAM2043S.
courses.course_outline: Topics include: the Fourier-transform solution of linear PDEs on the line; long-term asymptotic behaviour of solutions via the methods of Laplace, stationary phase and steepest descents; nonlinear waves including the method of characteristics, the effect of dissipation, the Cole-Hopf transform for the Burgers equation and travelling fronts for the KPP equation; the effect of dispersion including KdV and nonlinear Schrödinger equation; elliptic integrals and elliptic functions; dark and bright solitons; kinks and breathers for the sine-Gordon equation; multisoliton solutions via the Hirota method and Bäcklund transformations.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 3rd period.
courses.notes: Can be taken in conjunction with MAM3045S as lectures are arranged so that this is possible.
courses.dp_requirements: A class record of 30% or more.
courses.assessment: Class record counts up to 50%; the balance is given by the final examination.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM2040F, MAM2041F, MAM2042S and MAM2043S.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces students to the calculus of functions of one complex variable, with special focus on applications to other disciplines (e.g. Physics). Topics include: calculus of complex functions of one variable; conformal maps; complex contour integration and the Cauchy-Goursat theorem; Taylor and Laurent series of complex functions; calculus of residues; applications of complex contour integration to real-valued integrals; principal value of an integral; multi-valued complex functions; analytic continuation; and special functions.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 3rd period.
courses.notes: Can be taken in conjunction with MAM3042F as lectures are arranged so that this is possible.
courses.dp_requirements: A class record of 30% or more; satisfactory attendance at lectures and tutorials; submission of all assigned work.
courses.assessment: Class record counts up to 50%; the balance is made up by the final examination.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM2040F, MAM2041F, MAM2042S and MAM2043S.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces special relativity and general relativity, including: tensors; the metric tensor; symmetries; curvature; Einstein's field equations; and solutions of Minkowski space and Black Holes.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 3rd period.
courses.notes: Can be taken in conjunction with MAM3043S as lectures are arranged so that this is possible.
courses.dp_requirements: A class record of 30% or more.
courses.assessment: Class record counts up to 50%; the balance is given by the final examination.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: MAM2040F, MAM2041F, MAM2042S and MAM2043S.
courses.course_outline: Applications, description of fluids, equations of fluid flow for simple fluids, and analytical techniques.
courses.lecture_times: Monday and Tuesday, meridian. Thursday, 5th period.
courses.dp_requirements: A class record of 30% or more; attendance of both 75% of all lectures and 75% of all tutorials.
courses.assessment: Class record counts up to 50%; the balance is made up by the final examination.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.course_outline: With permission from the Convenor, and subject to the availability of a suitable supervisor in the Department, students may complete a project or internship on a topic in Applied Mathematics. This is strongly recommended for students intending to continue to Honours in Applied Mathematics.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: (i) 65% or higher for MAM3000W (or equivalent at another institution); and (ii) the average of the four marks for MAM1000W, MAM1019H, MAM2000W and MAM3000W (or equivalents) should be 65% or higher. Acceptance subject to individual approval by the Head of Department. Students are not allowed to repeat this course. Students are expected to complete the programme in one year.
courses.course_outline: This course provides an introduction to some topics basic to a professional mathematician. Students complete a mathematics project, at least three of four core modules in Algebra, Analysis, Differential Geometry and Topology, and other modules for a total of at least 160 credits. Most modules are 20 credits; the project (thesis and two seminars) is 40 credits. Typical additional module offerings include a selection from: Algebraic Geometry, Category Theory, Computational Complexity, Cryptology, Differential Topology, Functional Analysis, Graph Theory, Homological Algebra, Lie Algebras, Measure Theory, Number Theory, Operator Theory, Partial Differential Equations, and Theory of Hamiltonian Groups. Students may also pursue approved reading modules.
courses.notes: Course code MAM4000W carries no NQF credit value itself. Students are concurrently registered for MAM4013W (coursework component, 120 NQF credits) and MAM4014W (research project, 40 NQF credits). Students registered for MAM4000W are expected to tutor in the Department.
courses.assessment: Project counts 25% of the final mark and must be passed (50%). On average, the examination counts at least 50% of the balance. Three core modules together count 37.5% of the final mark. The remaining 37.5% is calculated using the student's best marks in other modules. Coursework and project codes must each be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: (i) 60% or higher for MAM3000W; and (ii) the average of the four marks for MAM1000W, MAM1019H, MAM2000W and MAM3000W should be 60% or higher. Acceptance subject to individual approval by the Head of Department. Students are not allowed to repeat this course.
courses.course_outline: This course, together with MAM4110W and MAM4014W, covers the content of MAM4000W over two years. Students do two core modules in the first semester and two more modules in the second semester (total 80 credits).
courses.assessment: As per MAM4000W.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: A pass in MAM4100W.
courses.course_outline: This course, together with MAM4100W and MAM4014W, covers the content of MAM4000W over two years. Students do two modules in the first semester and the project (MAM4014W) in the second semester (total 80 credits including MAM4014W).
courses.assessment: As per MAM4000W.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: (i) 65% or higher for MAM3040W (or equivalent at another institution); and (ii) the average of the three marks for MAM1043H/1044H, MAM2046W and MAM3040W (or equivalents) should be 65% or higher. Acceptance subject to individual approval by the Head of Department. Students are not allowed to repeat this course. Students are expected to complete the programme in one year.
courses.course_outline: This course provides an introduction to a selection of topics in applied mathematics. Students complete an applied mathematics project and modules for a total of at least 160 credits. Most modules are 20 credits; the project (thesis and two seminars) is 40 credits. Each student's curriculum must be approved by the convenor and must include a minimum of 60 credits of applied mathematics modules taught by MAM (CERECAM and DMTCS modules are considered in this category). Students are encouraged to include suitable modules from MAM4000W and from cognate departments (e.g. Computer Science, Physics, Statistics, Economics, Oceanography). Typical module offerings include a selection from: Advanced Mathematical Methods, Continuum Mechanics, Finite Element Analysis, Mathematical Biology, General Relativity and Cosmology, and String Theory.
courses.notes: Course code MAM4001W carries no NQF credit value itself. Students are concurrently registered for MAM4015W (coursework component, 120 NQF credits) and MAM4016W (research project, 40 NQF credits). Students are expected to tutor in the Department.
courses.assessment: Project and seminar together count 25% of the final mark and must be passed (50%). On average, the examination counts at least 50% of the balance. At least 37.5% of the final mark must come from applied mathematics modules taught by MAM. The remaining 37.5% is calculated using the student's best marks in other modules. Coursework and project codes must each be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: (i) 60% or higher for MAM3040W; and (ii) the average of the three marks for MAM1043H/1044H, MAM2046W and MAM3040W should be 60% or higher. Acceptance subject to individual approval by the Head of Department. Students are not allowed to repeat this course.
courses.course_outline: This course, together with MAM4111W and MAM4016W, covers the content of MAM4001W over two years. Students do two core modules in the first semester and two more modules in the second semester (total 80 credits).
courses.assessment: As per MAM4001W.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: A pass in MAM4101W.
courses.course_outline: This course, together with MAM4101W and MAM4016W, covers the content of MAM4001W over two years. Students do two modules in the first semester and the project (MAM4016W) in the second semester (total 80 credits including MAM4016W).
courses.assessment: As per MAM4001W.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.course_outline: This course consists of an investigation of an approved topic chosen for intensive study by the candidate, culminating in the submission of a dissertation. The dissertation shall demonstrate the successful completion of a programme of training in research methods, a thorough understanding of the scientific principles underlying the research and an appropriate acquaintance with the relevant literature. It must be clearly presented and conform to the standards of the department and faculty.
courses.assessment: Examination by dissertation.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.course_outline: The course consists of the investigation of one or two topics chosen for intensive study by the candidate and approved by the Head of Department. Examination is by dissertation; an oral examination may be required. The Department has research programmes in four particular areas of Applied Mathematics: (i) general relativity and astrophysics; (ii) mathematical modelling of biological, ecological and environmental systems; (iii) continuum mechanics, applied analysis and finite elements; and (iv) nonlinear evolution equations and non-integrable systems. Candidates are particularly encouraged to take part in one of these programmes.
courses.assessment: Examination by dissertation; oral examination may also be required.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.prerequisites: AST5003F.
courses.course_outline: Students work on an approved research topic in Astrophysics & Space Science on which a minor dissertation must be presented for formal examination. Part of the National Astrophysics & Space Science Programme (NASSP); for further details see the entry under the Department of Astronomy.
courses.assessment: The minor dissertation must be presented for formal examination.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.course_outline: This course consists of an investigation of an approved topic chosen for intensive study by the candidate, culminating in the submission of a dissertation. The dissertation shall demonstrate the successful completion of a programme of training in research methods, a thorough understanding of the scientific principles underlying the research and an appropriate acquaintance with the relevant literature. It must be clearly presented and conform to the standards of the department and faculty.
courses.assessment: Examination by dissertation.

courses.course_title: Generative AI for Research: Applications & Implications
courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.course_outline: This course introduces graduate science students to generative artificial intelligence and its applications in research processes. Students gain a comprehensive understanding of how AI systems work, their capabilities, limitations and ethical implications. The course covers practical applications of AI across the research lifecycle, including ideation, literature review, data exploration and scientific writing. Through hands-on workshops and critical discussion, students develop skills to effectively and responsibly integrate AI tools into their research practice while maintaining scientific integrity and addressing domain-specific challenges.

courses.department: Department of Mathematics and Applied Mathematics
courses.course_outline: The PhD is a research degree on an advanced topic under supervision. The thesis must constitute a substantial contribution to knowledge in the chosen subject, show evidence of original investigation and give a full statement of the literature on the subject. The PhD degree demands that the candidate is able to conduct independent research on their own initiative, demonstrating they are at the academic forefront in the topic selected, that the work is original and advances knowledge in the relevant field.
courses.assessment: Examination by thesis alone.
